content:
# Deuteronomy 20 — Disagreement Log

**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 20 (20 verses) | **Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents compared:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

## v.1 — "a people more numerous than you" (EMET) vs. "an army larger than yours" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "horses and chariots, a people more numerous than you" |
| LOGOS | "horses and chariots and an army larger than yours" |
| PNEUMA | "horses and chariots and an army far larger than yours" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ve-am rav mimekha* = "a people more numerous than you." EMET renders literally but "a people" in this military context is "an army." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "an army larger than yours" is contextually accurate. PNEUMA adds "far" (amplification). Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.2 — "the priest shall approach and speak" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "the priest shall step forward and address the troops" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the priest shall approach and speak to the people" |
| LOGOS | "the priest shall approach and speak to the people" |
| PNEUMA | "the priest shall step forward and address the troops" |

**Ruling:** PNEUMA: "troops" and "step forward" are dynamic paraphrases. Hebrew *ha-am* = "the people." Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.3 — Four verbs of fear

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Do not let your heart grow faint; do not be afraid, and do not panic, and do not be terrified" |
| LOGOS | "Let not your heart faint; do not be afraid or tremble or be in dread of them" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not lose heart or panic. Do not be terrified or filled with dread" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew has four distinct terms: *yirak levav* (heart softens/faints), *tir'u* (fear), *tachpazu* (panic/flee hastily), *tir'atsu* (dread/shatter). EMET renders "grow faint / afraid / panic / terrified." LOGOS renders "faint / afraid / tremble / be in dread." PNEUMA reduces to two pairs. Consensus follows LOGOS for its four-term preservation with slight adjustment for readability.

---

## v.4 — "to save you" (EMET) vs. "to give you victory" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to save you" |
| LOGOS | "to give you victory" |
| PNEUMA | "give you victory" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *le-hoshi'a etckem* = "to save/deliver you." In military context this is "give victory." Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "to give you victory."

---

## v.5–7 — Questions format: "Is there any man" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "Who is the man" (EMET)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Who is the man who has built a new house..." |
| LOGOS | "Is there any man who has built a new house..." |
| PNEUMA | "Is there anyone here who has built a new house..." |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *mi ha-ish asher* = "who is the man who." EMET's literal "Who is the man" is accurate. LOGOS reformulates as "Is there any man." PNEUMA adds "here." Consensus follows LOGOS as idiomatic and not adding to the text.

---

## v.6 — "enjoyed its fruit" vs. "made common use of it"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "has not yet made common use of it" |
| LOGOS | "has not enjoyed its fruit" |
| PNEUMA | "has not yet enjoyed its fruit" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ve-lo chilelo* = technical term for "releasing from first-fruit prohibition." EMET renders technically ("made common use of"); LOGOS/PNEUMA render the practical meaning ("enjoyed its fruit"). Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for accessibility.

---

## v.8 — "fearful and faint of heart" (EMET) vs. "fearful and fainthearted" (LOGOS) vs. "afraid or has lost his nerve" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "fearful and faint of heart" |
| LOGOS | "fearful and fainthearted" |
| PNEUMA | "afraid or has lost his nerve" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *yare ve-rakh ha-levav* = "afraid and soft of heart." LOGOS compounds it as "fainthearted" (one word); EMET retains "faint of heart." PNEUMA's "lost his nerve" is too colloquial. Consensus follows LOGOS ("fearful and fainthearted").

---

## v.9 — "commanders of armies" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "military commanders" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "commanders of the armies" |
| LOGOS | "commanders of armies" |
| PNEUMA | "military commanders" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *sarey tseva'ot* = "commanders of armies/hosts." Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.11 — "all the people found in it" (EMET) vs. "all the people who are found in it" (LOGOS) vs. "all the people in it" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "all the people found in it shall be subject to you as forced labor" |
| LOGOS | "all the people who are found in it shall do forced labor for you" |
| PNEUMA | "all the people in it shall serve as forced laborers for you" |

**Ruling:** Minor variation. Consensus follows LOGOS. Note: EMET's bracketed "[subject]" is unnecessary; "shall do forced labor for you" is the correct rendering of *le-mas ve-avadukha*.

---

## v.13 — "strike every male" (EMET) vs. "strike all its males" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "you shall strike every male in it with the edge of the sword" |
| LOGOS | "you shall strike all its males with the edge of the sword" |
| PNEUMA | "put every male to the sword" |

**Ruling:** LOGOS: "all its males" is equivalent to "every male." PNEUMA drops "with the edge of the sword" (*le-phi cherev*). Consensus follows LOGOS (preserving the idiom "with the edge of the sword").

---

## v.14 — "little ones" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "children" (PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the women and the little ones" |
| LOGOS | "the women and the little ones" |
| PNEUMA | "the women, children" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *ha-taf* = "the little ones/children." EMET/LOGOS: "little ones." Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

## v.16 — "any soul" (EMET) vs. "nothing that breathes" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "you shall not let any soul live" |
| LOGOS | "you shall save alive nothing that breathes" |
| PNEUMA | "you must not leave alive anything that breathes" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *kol neshama* = "every breath/soul." LOGOS/PNEUMA: "nothing that breathes" communicates the totality formula more naturally. Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.19 — "for many days" (EMET) vs. "for a long time" (LOGOS/PNEUMA)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "for many days" |
| LOGOS | "for a long time" |
| PNEUMA | "for a long time" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *yamim rabbim* = "many days." EMET is literal; LOGOS/PNEUMA idiomatic. Both are accurate. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA ("a long time").

---

## v.19 — Rhetorical question form

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "For is the tree of the field a man, that it should come against you in the siege?" |
| LOGOS | "For is the tree of the field a man, that it should be besieged by you?" |
| PNEUMA | "Is the tree a person, that you should besiege it?" |

**Ruling:** EMET: "come against you in the siege" reverses the siege relationship (MT: the tree is not a combatant you should besiege). LOGOS: "besieged by you" is more accurate. PNEUMA's abbreviation drops "of the field." Consensus follows LOGOS.

---

## v.20 — "not a food tree" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "not a tree for food" (EMET/LOGOS variant)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "not a food tree" |
| LOGOS | "not trees for food" |
| PNEUMA | "not fruit trees" |

**Ruling:** Hebrew *lo-ets ma'akhal* = "not a tree of food/eating." EMET: "not a food tree." LOGOS: "not trees for food." PNEUMA: "not fruit trees." Consensus follows LOGOS ("not food trees" — adjusting to plural to match context).

---

*SOPHIA — Deuteronomy 20 disagreements logged: 14*
